speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the 29th of March, 1995. Gershon, you were just speaking about the synagogue in Dessau. Could you just tell us more about anti-Semitic events that happened around that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as the war was beginning to break out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were many incidents. But I think we can-- without going into lots of details on the incidents-- the major event as far as the Jewish life and the synagogues, cultural things were concerned, not only in Dessau but in Germany generally, of course took place towards the end of 1938, in fact on the 9th of November '38, when a decree was made by the Nazi party to destroy all Jewish synagogues, burn the-- set fire to all the books, destroy Jewish shops, and whatever they could lay their hands on. It so happened, like I mentioned before, that I was in Dessau attending [HEBREW], and it also so happened that the [HEBREW], which I think I mentioned before was in the little room where Moses Mendelssohn was born, was opposite on the same street, opposite the synagogue. We heard a lot of commotion. We heard a lot of noise. Ran out, and there I saw people marching up to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of people, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, groups of people in uniform, both brown and black, meaning the SR and SS, and with mobs of others shouting, [GERMAN], which means 'death to the Jews.' And-- now, even though I was very well known in-- in Aken, in Dessau, I was like a young fellow, a young kid. I also had made it a habit to wear a clothing very similar to the Hitler Youth, which sort of protected me in a way. There wore black. I wore navy blue, but it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what you wore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were shorts and socks. You know, it was just-- you know, I had no insignias or anything like this, but the colors matched in, so I-- that I didn't stand out from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a Jew. And I followed those people, and of course what I saw was the synagogue being attacked with stones and all sorts of things, and-- and the [INAUDIBLE] being wrecked, the [INAUDIBLE] being pulled out. And there's many books been written about Kristallnacht. I don't think that I have to say [INAUDIBLE] barely to say that--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you actually experienced it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The place was burned. To my delight, I found out later on that two of the people actually got killed in the process. But this is just part of, you know, the-- of what went on. And after having watched this for a while, I got on my bike because I saw them moving over to another place which was also not far from there and destroying a shopfront. It then occurred to me that because I knew a lot of other people in the city, that I should at least try and warn them to get out and proceeded to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my own. So I got onto my bike. I went to a couple of places. I distinctly remember going to a place, number eight [GERMAN], where there was a Jewish furrier who had a-- and they were relatives, distant relatives. And I raced up the stairs and told them to get out and ask questions, which they did. And of course, within 15 minutes, the mob had arrived, and we could see them throwing the furniture out of the second floor, and the-- and the clothing and all this sort of thing and move on

speaker: interviewer
text: So after I'd been to-- I don't know how many, half a dozen, at least, places that I knew-- I thought, well, it's about time I got going, and-- it occurred-- before that, I didn't think about it, but it occurred to me time that I went home and found out what's happening at home. So I got on my bike, and I rode like crazy. It didn't take me an hour then to get back. And to my great relief, I found that nothing had happened at home. I'm talking about Aken. In your family home--

speaker: interviewee
text: In my family home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was at home when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, brother, and sister were at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not your father. He was working.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father was already gone. That's something we have to come back to because that's the other big event in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll come back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so nothing had happened. And I think this was either out of ignorance-- we didn't have an open shop. So there was not a, you know, an open shop to attack. Or that somebody-- you know, we were-- we had a few good friends. When I say 'good friends,' some friends-- some people that-- neighbors that we got on fairly well with, that they-- I don't know. I don't want to say protected us, but-- anyway, nothing happened. And I was very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to come back to the other thing was, of course, that during '38-- you asked me if my father was home. He wasn't home because during 1938-- I don't remember the exact date-- But well before Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to the best of my memory. It was, yes, because a decree was issued that all Polish nationals were to be arrested and sent back to the country of their birth, even if they had changed their nationality-- in other words, had become German citizen-- it wouldn't make any difference. They were to be sent back to the country of their birth, and in this case, it would have been Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father-- was he a Polish national? He-- did he change his nationality? Not that it mattered, [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was what they called [GERMAN]. He couldn't become a German, but he was also not a Polish anymore. So he was neither this nor that. He had a Polish passport, which had expired. So with that, finding out that he would be arrested, they [? came ?] to our door and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out? Was that announced on the news, in the newspapers, or people told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. This-- this was a-- no, no, this was a decree that was announced. We didn't want him to come home. So I knew where he was, and I got onto my bike, which was in my mode of transport. And I met him at the train station, a place called Kothen, which was another 15 kilometers in the other area, and told him what the situation was, that in the meantime, the Gestapo had been searching for him at our house and that he shouldn't come home. And he didn't. He never came home and-- except on one occasion where he did, and they came and searched for him. He then hid in a cupboard and lucky for us, on that particular occasion, they didn't go right through the whole place. And that was the last time he ever came back to-- to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the gap there, between the time he stayed away to the time that he stopped--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was a number of month. I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: A number of months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any contact at all with your mother, yourself, or your brother and sister in that time? Did you manage to see him anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him a couple of times in different places. But it had become very dangerous because they used to come on a fairly regular basis to our house looking for him. They obviously has also staked out a place to see-- you know, to try and catch him. And at one occasion I remember very clearly, they came, and they-- they thought, well, the wife must know why-- my mother must know where he is and must know or something. There must be some contact because, you know, she's living, she's getting a-- she's got an income from somewhere. Where's the money coming from, this, that, and the other. And so they said, OK, we're going to-- we're going to take your son, which was me. And so they dragged me off to jail. It was not in order to keep me. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that? They told you that? What-- how did all--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it was to force my mother to speak, all right? And they finished up keeping me for a couple of days. She didn't say anything, and I-- I really don't think she knew where he was at that particular time. So she couldn't have told them anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't really know and-- I did and I didn't. I knew where I would have been able to contact him, you know? So-- but I didn't say anything either. So they kept me for a couple of days, and they let me go. I wasn't being treated very badly, although I can't say I particularly enjoyed [LAUGHS] being locked up. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were actually in a jail cell in Aken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So the time came that there was no life to continue this way. Now, I mentioned before that my mother had to live off something. So what happened was that because my-- the nature of my father's business was a time payment business, I, now, at the age of 12 and 1/2 or whatever it was-- fell on me to go around and do the rounds, which I knew because I had been going with him since a couples of years before that. I knew all the customers. I knew the lists and that, and now it fell on me to go around and start attempting to collect some of the money that was that was due to us. And of course the result was positive in some cases, negative in others-- 'negative' meaning the people that owed us money, if they didn't want to pay, they just slammed the door in my face, or they might just call out some insults and, you know, threaten, don't come back here. And others were very nice and continued to pay, and it was through that that we were able to-- to buy our groceries, if you like, you know, to-- to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did those rounds on-- on your bike.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And brought the money home to your mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I kept on going to Dessau, and then my thirteenth year approached. Now, I don't know whether I have this all in-- in the right--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. That doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, might be going a little bit backwards and forward.

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Time came for my bar mitzvah. Now, my father wasn't there. There was nobody in-- in-- I mean, the rabbi had-- was gone. The community was a community made up of a handful of people that were running a-- more or less an underground service. But they arranged a-- a bar mitzvah for me. And-- do I have to explain what a bar mitzvah is? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you don't have to explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was so under--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you can describe it to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: --under those circumstances. Well, I only remember that there were-- there was a minyan there. There were ten people there of all ages. And I said my part that I had been taught before. It was a rather sad situation because it's a sort of thing that children look forward to. It's not just the fact that you become an adult. An adult I had become already two years before. But it's something that children look forward to. It's like a birthday party that-- you know, you expect a-- they expect a fountain pen or something like that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course. Did you have a little party?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, there was no time for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any presents--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing--

speaker: interviewer
text: --at all, any gifts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was nothing like that. I mean, the fact was that I had this bar mitzvah, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall whether the bar mitzvah was before-- you were born in '26, and the war broke out September 1, '39. Was the bar mitzvah before that or after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember. I mean, I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: --connect-- connect it to-- to this. You know, when times-- in retrospect-- first of all, you're likely to forget. You-- you can get times mixed up. And lots of and lots of events that you're talking about today happened that when you live them, you don't pay particular notice that it happens to be on the 9th of November or that it happens to be at any other date. It just happens to be today. We're doing this interview today. But if you ask me in three years' time what date did we do it, I doubt whether I will remember it. I will remember the interview, but I won't remember the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you remember the September 1, '39-- do you remember the war actually being announced that it was broken out-- the Germans marching into Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember that at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't. We were busy living our lives. And I guess I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see your father at all after that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Of course, what happened after that was that my father then got desperate, and he tried to escape from Germany. And he, with a number of other men, tried to cross the Belgian border to-- to get out. Unfortunately-- and this was a-- he wasn't the only one. There were thousands of people trying to get out from all sorts of borders. In his case, it just happened to be the Belgian border. And they got caught. They got caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did they get caught by?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the border police.

speaker: interviewer
text: German border police.

speaker: interviewee
text: German border police. After being interrogated, the local guy who was in charge decided that-- seeing that my father was the only one amongst the guys there who had a Polish passport, even though it was expired-- he--

speaker: interviewer
text: He had that on him, did he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He couldn't keep him because he was a foreign national. Now, he-- that was in-- he was quite wrong because in fact, he was more wanted than the German passport-holders, Jewish German passport-- you understand what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand what you're saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he let him go. With that, he disappeared again. And-- and-- just something came to my mind then that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Relevant to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. It's relevant, yes. It just came to my mind. He had a gold-fob watch with him. And so that he wouldn't get caught and be accused of trying to smuggle gold out of the country, he went and threw that watch onto that wagon that was transporting them. And I don't know how the story-- how the whole thing went, but after the war, somehow he got this-- you know, there was some connection made that the watch came home, and we got that watch back. And I still have it today. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have no idea how that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got no idea how this person that knew-- knew where to send it to. But I've still got it. And that just came back to my mind because we were talking about the reasons for-- for escaping. Anyway, he then went back to Berlin. And this was in 1939. And at that particular point, there were people being organized by the Jewish-- what do you call it, [NON-ENGLISH]-- the Jewish congregation to help people that are in danger to get out. And he was told to come to a certain place at a certain time. And they went on a transport out from Germany to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a Jewish group?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a Jewish group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he, approximately, at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he would've been about 40. Born in '99, so he would have been about 40. This group arrived in England the day the war broke out. Now,--

speaker: interviewer
text: For the purpose of this recording, you heard all this later, didn't you? You were not aware at all what happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wasn't aware of that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no contact whatsoever.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This is what I know since. And if you want me to just talk briefly about that part--

speaker: interviewer
text: I would.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not my story, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's an important one.

speaker: interviewee
text: He, with quite a number of others, arrived in England. On the day or the second day after their arrival, they were all arrested on Churchill's instructions that all people that had come from Germany were to be declared enemy aliens, or at least that's how I was told the story went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or arrested as Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: As enemy aliens. Didn't matter whether they were Jews or not Jews. And in fact, some were Jews-- most of them were Jews. Some were non-Jews. They were all arrested because they reckoned they could all be spies, and they didn't have time to sort them out. And they were sent to a camp in [NON-ENGLISH], then from there, into another camp. And as time went on-- and so they lived in that prisoner-of-war camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a detainment camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place where they lived in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you-- would you-- [NON-ENGLISH]. OK. No, that's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK? Then as time went on and the war proceeded and Germany looked like threatening England, and in fact, they might try and invade England, the English got worried that they might free those prisoners. I don't know what they were going to do, but they were going to free them. Maybe they were going to start shooting with broomsticks. I don't know. So they-- they deported them to the Isle of Man. And they sat there for a while. And then when Germany really looked, and in fact made an attempt to invade England, and that got too-- these people got too dangerous for the English to keep there, they decided to divide them up. And by that time, there were about 3,000 or more prisoners there. And they split them up into two ships. One, I believe, went to Canada and was sunk, and the other one was sent to Australia. It was a troop ship, or converted troop ship, called the Dunera, and that is a separate story. There's been a film made about the Dunera. There's a memorial here in Sydney to it. There's a Dunera Association.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father would've been one of the Dunera boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was one of the Dunera boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he arrived in Sydney and was then sent to [? Hay, ?] to Tura. And sat out-- not sat out the war years. He was there for a couple of years, and then they were given jobs in the armaments industry or whatever, you know, help they could do-- factories. And that's what he did for he last-- till the end of the war. So this is just a by-the-way story.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but it's a very important story. Is there anything else you can say about your father?

words.text: you can say about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the important part there is that during that time, he got to hear from my late mother that I had arrived in Palestine, as it was. But we'll come back to that--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come back to that. That's another story. What was his-- was there any contact with him and your mother before that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, there was contact with my mother through the Red Cross and also all the mail. And of course, every letter was opened.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Aken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First at the old address in Aken, and then our home-- the previous year this was taken, given to somebody else, and she was put into a different location, but still in Aken. And he corresponded with her for as long as she was there, which was 194-- probably beginning of '42. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll come now to talk about still when your family-- your mother and yourself and your brother and sister-- lived in Aken and when you first moved from your original home. Just before we come to that, though, could you just mention when your father-- when did your father first make contact with your mother there? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can't tell you exactly because we have to come back to the story. We-- we sort of went out to my father's side. But now let's get back to Aken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Aken, when you lived in Aken and you collected--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what happened then was my mother was put under house arrest, meaning that she could leave the house but she couldn't travel. She had to get special permission to travel. And then a scheme was put into place. I don't exactly know the origins of it, but it was a beginning of an organization of collecting Jewish males at certain times, and Jewish children and separating families and all. Under those situations, we were advised that I would be sent to a-- how will I describe it-- a youth hostel in Hamburg. You want me to spell it for you?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can if you like.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHING] I think anybody knows Hamburg. And that is exactly what took place. So I went to Hamburg. And in Hamburg I was put into this-- we used to call it in Hebrew [HEBREW], which means the youth house. It was also described as a [HEBREW], meaning a 'getting ready'-- I can say it in German-- but getting ready for getting ready. [HEBREW] was really a movement where young people used to go to prepare themselves for aliyah to Israel or Palestine. Now, because we were far too young for that, [? they would ?] say this was the [HEBREW]. This was a-- the-- getting prepared for being prepared. You know, it's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the fact was that we were now 13 years of age. We had a few girls that were a little bit older, but altogether there were about 55 kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: From?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over Germany, in this home. And the regime boss-- we were-- in charge was a youth leader, a Jewish person, who was in fact the son of a Hamburg rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just as we're getting into that, so you actually left your home in Aken, your original home in Aken, to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mum and your brother and sister were still living in Aken, and you were just notified that that's where you were going to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, not long after that, my sister got the same advice, and she was sent to a similar institution in a place called Magdeburg. Now, you may remember Magdeburg was very prominent recently in the overthrow of the German communist regime. So this is just bringing it up-to-date to tell you where it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went-- a couple of years [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And this was sort of the thing that they were doing and separating families, and she was sent there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. A few months later. But I'll stay with my story. And so we lived there. And being 13, or between the age of 13 and 14, we started going to school. There was a Jewish day school in Hamburg. And the idea was that we'd go to school half a day, and we would, you know, do social study or whatever for the rest of the time. And so we started--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue practicing religious study as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, but not to the effect-- you know, we-- we kept some-- we were getting further and further away from it because there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you practice Shabbat, observe Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was just not the opportunity to do these things. And we had amongst our children-- the children came from all over Germany. And there were some of them that didn't even know they were Jewish. They came there simply because the authorities had decided that their great-grandfather was Jewish or their great-grandmother was Jewish, and therefore they are Jewish. And Hitler's decree was you have to go back three generations or whatever to get rid of all the Jews. So they were made Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, we'll--
